topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. Liam coughed, waving a hand in front of his face, his eyes scanning the haphazard stacks of boxes. His mother, Eleanor, had always been meticulous about her filing, organized to the point of obsession, but this… this was different. He pulled out a shoebox labeled ‘2018-2022.’ Inside, nestled between old photographs and dried flowers, were pristine, folded sheets of paper. Math proofs, essays on Shakespeare, diagrams of cellular structures—every assignment from high school, neatly cataloged. Liam picked up a crumpled worksheet for history. It was exactly how she’d left it on her desk, perfectly preserved. He turned the paper over, his brow furrowing. He’d always assumed she was just sentimental, a bit overly fussy, but this level of preservation felt… excessive. He tapped the paper against his thigh, a slow, deliberate rhythm. "What is this, Mom?" he asked, his voice tight. The silence that followed was heavy, weighted with unspoken questions.

[story 2]

Maya approached the mahogany desk in the study, the room feeling too quiet, too formal. She was looking for a misplaced set of keys, but instead, she found a hidden compartment behind a loose panel. Inside, a meticulously labeled file cabinet contained binders overflowing with schoolwork. Physics notes, poetry analyses, even rough drafts of term papers. Maya blinked, rereading a note dated freshman year. It was her own handwriting, but the tone was strangely detached, almost clinical. She ran a finger over the spine of a textbook. A strange tension coiled in her stomach. She pulled out a test from sophomore year chemistry. The precision of the handwriting was unnerving. She flipped through several pages, her breath catching in her throat. Why would her mother hoard every piece of academic output? Maya backed away slowly, her gaze fixed on the stack of papers, a knot tightening in her chest. She needed an explanation, but the words wouldn't form.

[story 3]

David found the collection tucked away in the back of his grandmother’s old wardrobe. It was a trove of paper, a chronological record of his academic life, from middle school through college. He picked up a geometry assignment, the ink crisp and dark. He turned it over, his fingers tracing the lines of the problems. He felt a prickle of unease crawl up his arms. He remembered his mother, always stressed about deadlines, always frantic about forgetting things. This level of archiving felt almost theatrical. David sat on the dusty floor, letting the silence of the room amplify the strangeness. He picked up an essay from his first year of university. The prose was flawless, almost impersonal. He shook his head slightly, a faint tremor running through him. He wanted to dismiss it as an elaborate prank, a bizarre hobby, but the sheer volume and perfection of the collection made him hesitate, his jaw working silently as he weighed the implications of what he was seeing.
